text: The surgeon elevates the transverse colon and omentum, displacing the small bowel to the right upper quadrant. The assistant holds the sigmoid colon up, allowing the surgeon to make a peritoneal incision on the sigmoid mesentery near the sacral promontory. The incision follows a J-shaped path towards the pelvis and cranially up to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle. Mobilization proceeds from medial to lateral, identifying the left hypogastric nerve, which is dropped down. The dissection plane lies close to the mesocolon, hugging the IMA.

The surgeon dissects and divides the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) below its origin, followed by the dissection and division of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The medial dissection continues to the white line, while the ureter and gonadal vessels remain obscured by the doll's fascia. Posterior rectal dissection is performed in the mesorectal plane, with caution taken to avoid injury to the pre-sacral fascia.

The surgeon completes the posterior dissection at the SIII level by sharply dividing the recto-sacral fascia. The dissection plane is anterior to the loose areolar tissue, maintaining an intact mesorectum. A gauze is placed beneath the sigmoid mesocolon to guide the lateral dissection, which is then commenced, ensuring both dissection planes meet.

The assistant provides counter traction during lateral dissection on the sidewall, ensuring controlled mobilization of the descending colon to prevent redundancy. The mesocolon is marked at the site of colonic division, and the surgeon divides the sigmoid colon after demarcation.

The uterus is elevated to enhance anterior exposure. The surgeon begins lateral dissection of the rectum, with one assistant using a grasper to pull the rectum out of the pelvis while another provides counter traction on the pelvic sidewall. Circumferential mobilization of the rectum is performed, ensuring the endopelvic fascia on the lateral and pelvic sidewall remains intact to protect nerves and the mesorectum. The dissection planes on both sides are joined anteriorly.
The surgeon divides the lateral ligaments and dissects the rectum off the vagina, carefully dividing the rectovaginal septum while keeping the endopelvic fascia on the levator ani muscle intact.

The surgeon performs a digital rectal examination to confirm the adequacy of the caudal dissection. The final coccygeal and natal ligaments are divided, with the levator ani muscle visible as the boundary on all sides. The intersphincteric plane is carefully avoided. The sigmoid colon is divided using an endogia stapler, and the colonic reach for a colostomy is visually verified.
